title: SOLDERS-0002 — solders.rpc.responses.batch_to_json() .unwrap()s a serde deserialize error -> panic on a non-response element (crates/rpc-responses/src/lib.rs:2044)
panic_reason: called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: Error("invalid type: integer `N`, expected a map", line: 1, column: 2)
expected: a Python TypeError/ValueError naming the bad element, not a PanicException
guarded_twin: the 139 *Resp.from_json classmethods in the same solders build: they round-trip through serde and ?-propagate the deserialise Result (returning a clean Python error on a non-response), where batch_to_json alone .unwrap()s it. The fix = ?-propagate / map_err(PyValueError::new_err(...)) as the from_json family already does.
confirmed_build: solders 0.28.0 (target: CPython 3.14.4+ debug build, GIL on)
prior_art: specific site UNREPORTED; the PyO3 panic-on-bad-input class IS known (solders #122/#108/#91) and fixed once (PR #93, Keypair.from_base58_string). See notes/prior-art.md
notes: solders.rpc.responses.batch_to_json(seq) batch-serialises a sequence of RPC response objects; it iterates seq and round-trips each element through serde (deserialising it as a response = a JSON map) before serialising, and `.unwrap()`s that deserialise Result at crates/rpc-responses/src/lib.rs:2044. A non-empty iterable whose elements are not responses -- a bare integer `[0]`, or a byte from `b"a"` (which iterates to 97) -- makes the deserialise return Err(invalid type: integer N, expected a map) and the `.unwrap()` panics -> pyo3_runtime.PanicException. batch_to_json([]) / b"" are fine (no elements); batch_to_json('a') raises a proper TypeError. Elusive originally because the module parsers (parse_websocket_message/parse_notification/batch_from_json) and all 139 *Resp.from_json classmethods return a CLEAN error on a bare integer -- only batch_to_json unwraps. Surfaced 435x via the --new-uninit discovery sweep (module callables x hostile arg combos incl. a stateful bomb whose __str__ gives the varying integers 97/116/... in the panic reasons). Fix = propagate the serde error as a PyErr (`?`/map_err) instead of `.unwrap()`, or validate each element up front.
